data.title: OPC's core agent: Learning from AI. That's the conclusion.
data.description:
Author: Zhang Feng

When generative AI is no longer just a "tool" but evolves into an "intelligent assistant"; when "one-person companies" move from niche experiments to the mainstream narrative of the digital economy, the combination of OPC (one-person company/super individual) and intelligent agents is fundamentally reshaping the form of business organizations.

In fact,The relationship between OPC and intelligent agents is far more than a simple superposition of "technology + business," but a deep binding of "legal subjects and technological tools," and a systemic synergy of "carbon-based intelligence and silicon-based capabilities."

Author: Zhang Feng

In short: OPCs are "independent operating entities" in a legal and commercial sense, assuming decision-making, responsibility, and profits; Agents are "automated execution units" in a technological sense, responsible for efficiency, capability, and scale. Together, they constitute the "smallest, most flexible, and most efficient" operating loop in the digital economy era, and have become a focus of attention in the policy, industry, and legal fields. This article will systematically dissect the inherent logic of OPC and Intelligent Agent from five dimensions: conceptual clarification, core relationships, business value, compliance boundaries, and future trends. It aims to combine professional depth with practical reference, helping entrepreneurs, service providers, and legal professionals accurately grasp the core essence of their combination and seize the structural opportunities of the digital economy. I. Concepts: Understanding OPC and Agent within the Same Cognitive Dimension To understand the relationship between the two, we first need to dispel the misconception of conceptual confusion—OPC and Agent belong to two different fields, "business and law" and "technology and execution," respectively, yet they form an inseparable bond in the digital economy. (I) OPC: The “Minimum Independent Business Entity” in the AI ​​Era While OPC is often simply understood as a “sole limited liability company,” in the AI ​​era, its connotation has transcended the traditional legal definition, becoming a typical form of a “super individual”—a business entity led by a single natural person, with a team typically controlled to fewer than 15 people, achieving a closed-loop business model through AI tools. Its core attributes are reflected in three levels: Legal Level: OPCs possess independent legal person status (or legitimate commercial entity qualifications), have independent property and decision-making power, and can independently sign contracts, bear civil liability, and enjoy operating profits. After the implementation of the new Company Law in 2024, the restrictions on the establishment of OPCs were further relaxed, allowing a natural person to establish multiple OPCs and subsidiaries, providing a legal basis for the large-scale development of OPCs. At the same time, the law clarifies the boundaries of liability—shareholders bear limited liability up to the amount of their subscribed capital, but if there is commingling of assets or financial irregularities, it may trigger the "piercing the corporate veil," and shareholders will bear joint and several liability. From a business perspective: The core of OPC is "lightweight and high efficiency." It eliminates the need for complex organizational structures, large teams, and high fixed costs, relying on AI tools to complete the entire process from product development and marketing to customer service and financial management. This model enables OPC to respond quickly to market changes, significantly lowering the barriers to entrepreneurship and becoming an important path for ordinary people to start businesses in the AI ​​era. Policies in many regions also support this, with cities like Dongguan, Guangzhou, Wuhan, and Shenzhen introducing measures such as subsidies, space support, and scenario opening, covering the entire lifecycle of OPC development. From a fundamental perspective: The core of OPC is a "carbon-based decision-making center." Its leader (a natural person) is responsible for grasping the business direction, formulating strategies, and controlling risks. Its core value lies in human judgment, creativity, and empathy—capabilities that current AI cannot replace. The existence of OPC solves the problem of "AI tools lacking a decision-making body and a responsible entity," enabling technological capabilities to truly translate into commercial value. (II) Intelligent Agent: An "Automated Execution Unit" in the AI ​​Era. An intelligent agent is an AI program or system trained based on large models, algorithms, and data, capable of autonomously perceiving the environment, analyzing needs, planning paths, executing tasks, and achieving self-iteration. Unlike traditional AI tools, the core advantages of Agents lie in their "autonomy" and "collaboration"—they can autonomously complete complex tasks based on preset goals and environmental changes without real-time human commands. Multiple Agents can even collaborate to form an "intelligent agent cluster," accomplishing tasks that traditionally require a team. From a technical perspective: Agents possess four core capabilities—perception (automatically collecting and analyzing external information), planning (breaking down tasks and defining paths), execution (completing specific tasks such as content generation, customer communication, and order processing), and iteration (optimizing algorithms based on execution results). For example, an e-commerce Agent can autonomously complete the entire process of product selection, listing, customer service, order tracking, and data analysis, significantly reducing human intervention. Application Level: Agents are typical capability extension tools. They handle repetitive, procedural, and large-scale tasks that humans are "unwilling to do, unable to do well, or slow to do," freeing people from tedious tasks and allowing them to focus on creative and decision-making work. Entrepreneurs can use agents to complete market research, copywriting, and customer follow-up, while lawyers can use agents to complete case searches, contract review, and legal document drafting. Fundamental Level: Agents are "silicon-based execution centers," lacking independent legal status, decision-making capabilities, and legal liability. Their core value lies in technological efficiency—rapid response, precise execution, and continuous iteration. The emergence of Agents has solved the problem of "limited capabilities and insufficient efficiency of OPC," enabling an individual to achieve the operational effect of "one person doing the work of a whole team" through technology. (III) Core Consensus: Complementary Symbiosis, Not Competition OPC and Agents are not on the same dimension and do not have a competitive relationship. Instead, they are complementary symbiotic relationships of "subject and tool," "decision-making and execution," and "carbon-based and silicon-based." OPC solves the problems of "who makes decisions, who bears responsibility, and who profits," while Agents solve the problems of "how to execute efficiently, how to amplify capabilities, and how to reduce costs." Without Agents, OPCs are merely traditional one-person companies, unable to break through the limits of individual capabilities; without OPCs, Agents are merely technical tools lacking direction and carriers, unable to be transformed into commercial value. The combination of the two constitutes the most competitive business model in the AI ​​era. II. Symbiosis: The binding relationship between OPC and Agent can be divided into four layers, progressing from basic to core, from technology to law. (I) First Layer: Subject and Tool — OPC (Operator) — OPC — Dominant, Agent Executing — This is the most basic relationship: OPC, as an independent business entity, owns, controls, and directs Agent; Agent, as a technical tool, obeys instructions and executes business tasks. The dominant power lies with OPC, and the executive power lies with Agent. OPC's dominant role is now decision-making (determining direction and goals), control (setting Agent usage boundaries and permissions), and attribution (all execution results belong to OPC). The Agent efficiently completes tasks in various stages such as R&D, marketing, operations, and compliance. From a legal perspective, this relationship clarifies the attribution of responsibility—the Agent is not a legal entity, and all consequences of its actions are borne by the OPC that controls it. Whether it's infringement, breach of contract, or violation of regulations, the responsible party is the OPC. This is the core bottom line that the policy and legal communities are concerned about. (II) Second Layer: Capability Amplification – Agent Enables OPC to Achieve “One Person Equals a Team” This is the core value of the combination of the two, and also the essential difference between “AI-native OPC” and traditional one-person companies. Traditional one-person companies are limited by an individual's time, energy, and professional skills, resulting in a very limited operational ceiling. Agents, however, break this limitation: **Efficiency Amplification:** Agents can work 24/7 without pay. A single customer service agent can handle hundreds of clients simultaneously, achieving more than ten times the efficiency of a human agent; a copywriting agent can generate high-quality content in minutes, producing hundreds of pieces daily. **Capability Extension:** Agents enable OPCs to possess cross-disciplinary capabilities. Founders without programming skills can use code agents to develop websites; those without financial knowledge can use financial agents to handle bookkeeping and tax filing; and those without foreign language skills can use translation agents to conduct cross-border business. Scalability: A single OPC can deploy multiple agents simultaneously to work collaboratively, forming an "intelligent agent cluster," enabling large-scale operations that traditionally require a team. For example, an e-commerce OPC can deploy agents for product selection, product listing, customer service, and data analysis to automate the entire process. Without agents, there is no truly meaningful "AI-native OPC"; without OPC, agents are difficult to transform into sustainable commercial value. (III) Third Layer: Attribution of Responsibility — OPC Paying the Price for Agent's Actions As the bond between the two deepens, attribution of responsibility becomes the most pressing issue for policymakers and legal professionals. The core principle is clear: The Agent does not possess independent legal person status, and the legal consequences of all its actions are borne by the OPC, which controls it. Civil liability: The OPC is responsible for all contracts signed by the Agent, debts incurred, and tort losses caused by the Agent. If the Agent misuses data or executes incorrectly, the OPC is liable for compensation. Furthermore, the new Company Law requires OPC shareholders to prove their independent assets; otherwise, they will bear joint and several liability for the company's debts. Administrative Liability: If an Agent violates regulations regarding data compliance, algorithm compliance, or other regulatory requirements, the regulatory authorities will penalize the OPC. For example, if an Agent conducts generative AI services without registration, the OPC will face fines and orders to rectify the situation. Criminal Liability: If an Agent is used to commit fraud, infringe on citizens' personal information, or other criminal acts, the OPC founder or relevant responsible persons will bear criminal liability. Even if an Agent violates regulations autonomously without human instruction, the OPC still bears responsibility—as the controller, it has an obligation to regulate the Agent's algorithms, behavioral boundaries, and risk control mechanisms. This requires the OPC to establish a sound compliance management system when using Agents. (IV) Fourth Layer: Organizational Evolution—Reconstructing the Underlying Logic of Business Organizations The combination of these two aspects has driven the evolution of business organizations from a "traditional hierarchical system" to a "lightweight, flat, and intelligent" system. Organizational Structure: Traditional enterprises follow a multi-layered structure of "Company → Department → Employee → Tool," resulting in high costs. The combination of OPC and Agent forms a flat structure of "OPC → Agent Cluster → Task Execution," leading to highly efficient decision-making and extremely low costs. An e-commerce OPC can achieve full-process operation with just one founder and multiple Agents, at a cost only one-tenth or even one-hundredth of that of a traditional company. Production Relations: The traditional "employer-employee" relationship is replaced by a new relationship of "carbon-based decision-making entity - silicon-based execution unit." The relationship between OPC and Agent is one of "control and being controlled" and "using and being used," enabling individuals to operate independently without relying on an organization. Collaborative Model: Multiple OPCs can collaborate through Agent clusters to form an "OPC alliance," jointly completing complex projects and achieving resource complementarity and synergistic advantages. This collaborative model will become the mainstream form of the future digital economy. III. Value: Unlocking New Opportunities in the Digital Economy The deep integration of OPC and Agent brings significant value to entrepreneurs, industry service providers, and the development of the digital economy. (I) For Entrepreneurs: Low Cost, High Returns Traditional entrepreneurship requires substantial capital, manpower, and space, resulting in high risk and high barriers to entry. Under the OPC+Agent model, entrepreneurs only need a small amount of start-up capital, eliminating the need to rent space or hire employees, and can leverage Agents to achieve full-process operation. For example, content creators can utilize copywriting, design, and publishing agents to achieve large-scale content production and distribution with tens of thousands of yuan in start-up capital, significantly reducing entrepreneurial risk. Simultaneously, the efficient execution of Agents enables OPC to quickly seize market share and achieve high returns. (II) For Industry Service Providers: Expanding Scenarios and Building Differentiated Competitiveness Lawyers, tax professionals, technology service providers, incubators, and others can expand into new service scenarios based on the needs of OPC and Agent. For example, lawyers can launch lightweight compliance service packages covering OPC compliance self-inspection, Agent behavior compliance guidance, and contract template customization; technology service providers can develop Agent management platforms and multi-agent collaborative systems; and incubators can create dedicated "OPC+Agent" spaces to provide full-chain services and enhance their core competitiveness. (III) On the Digital Economy: Cultivating New Drivers and Promoting Changes in Production Relations OPCs are numerous and highly flexible, enabling rapid application of AI and digital technologies, fostering new business models such as AI-native services, lightweight e-commerce, and digital content creation. Simultaneously, the combination of OPCs and Agents breaks down traditional corporate organizational boundaries, making "everyone can start a business and everyone can participate" possible, thus forming a higher-quality and more sustainable digital economy ecosystem. IV. Boundaries: Core Risks and Prevention Recommendations While the combination of OPC and Agent brings opportunities, it also carries risks related to liability attribution, data compliance, algorithm compliance, and financial, tax, and labor matters, which require serious attention. (I) Core Compliance Risks: The lack of an agent behavior oversight mechanism leads to OPC bearing responsibility for violations and infringements; commingling of shareholder assets triggers the "piercing the corporate veil" mechanism, resulting in joint liability. Data Compliance Risks: Agents illegally collect, use, and store personal information or public data, violating the Personal Information Protection Law and the Data Security Law; training data contains issues such as infringement and lack of anonymization. Algorithm Compliance Risks: Agent algorithms may contain biases or vulnerabilities, leading to unfair execution results or losses; generative AI agents may lack algorithm registration and security assessment. Financial, Tax, and Employment Risks: Failure to conduct financial accounting and tax declarations as required; failure to distinguish between labor and employment relationships when outsourcing agent work to individuals, potentially resulting in penalties such as social security breaches and double wages. (II) Compliance and Prevention Recommendations: Complete registration and define the business scope; establish an independent financial management system and retain audit reports; establish an agent behavior supervision mechanism to monitor the execution process and retain records. Strengthen Data Compliance: Ensure the legality of data sources and prohibit the illegal crawling and use of un-anonymized data; strictly comply with the Personal Information Protection Law and implement obligations such as informed consent, data anonymization, and secure storage; cross-border data requires a security assessment or standard contract filing. **Standardize Algorithm Usage:** Generative AI Agents intended for public use must complete algorithm registration and security assessment; algorithms should be regularly tested and optimized, and design documents, training data sources, and execution records should be retained. **Standardize Financial, Tax, and Employment Practices:** Tax declarations should be made in accordance with regulations, ensuring invoice compliance and accurate cost listings, and fully enjoying tax incentives for small and micro enterprises; rights and obligations should be clearly defined during external collaborations, distinguishing between service and employment relationships. V. Trends: Standardization, Scalability, and Ecosystem Development With the iteration of AI technology and the improvement of policies, the collaboration between OPC and Agent will exhibit three major trends: Regulatory Standardization: Policies will further improve the behavioral norms, responsibility attribution, and compliance standards of Agents, establish a classified and precise regulatory mechanism, and introduce standards for algorithm auditing, data compliance, and accountability, promoting collaborative development towards compliance. Scalable Applications: Agent application scenarios will extend from e-commerce, content creation, and technology development to more fields such as finance, healthcare, education, and government affairs; multi-agent collaborative systems will become mainstream, further amplifying OPC capabilities, and support policies will also favor the "OPC+Agent" model. Ecosystem Collaboration: A collaborative ecosystem of "OPC+Agent+Industry Service Providers" will be formed, with service providers offering precise services based on OPC and Agent needs; OPCs will cooperate closely through Agent clusters, driving the continuous improvement of the digital economy ecosystem. VI. Outlook: Synergy Between Carbon-Based and Silicon-Based Technologies Ushering in a New Future for the Digital Economy The relationship between OPC and Agent is a typical portrayal of "subject and tool" and "decision-making and execution" in the AI ​​era, and even more so, a systemic synergy between "carbon-based intelligence" and "silicon-based capabilities." As the smallest independent business entity, OPC provides Agent with decision-making, responsibility, and a commercial vehicle; Agent, as an automated execution unit, provides OPC with efficiency, capabilities, and scalability. The combination of the two not only lowers the barriers to entrepreneurship and expands business boundaries, but also fundamentally restructures the logic of business organizations, driving profound changes in production relations. In a policy environment that encourages innovation while strengthening compliance, the synergistic development of OPC and Agent presents both tremendous opportunities and clear compliance requirements. Entrepreneurs need to accurately grasp the relationship between the two, adhere to compliance bottom lines, and leverage Agents to amplify their capabilities; industry service providers need to accurately connect with demand and create suitable service products; and the policy and legal communities need to continuously improve regulatory details, seeking a balance between innovation and risk. In the future, with the dual evolution of technology and regulations, the synergy between OPC and intelligent agents will become the mainstream form of the digital economy, potentially reshaping the smallest unit of the digital economy and truly ushering in a new business era of "carbon-based decision-making and silicon-based execution," injecting continuous momentum into the high-quality development of the digital economy.

data.title: a16z: Empowering the Future of On-Chain Markets - The Crucial Role of Predictability
data.description:
Authors: Pranav Garimidi, Joachim Neu, Max Resnick, a16z crypto researchers; Source: a16z crypto; Translated by: Shaw & Jinse Finance

Blockchain today can confidently claim that it possesses the processing power needed to compete with existing financial infrastructure. Current production systems can process tens of thousands of transactions per second, and future performance will see orders of magnitude improvement.

However, beyond sheer throughput, financial applications also require predictability. When a transaction is initiated—whether it's a trade, an auction, or an option exercise—reliably ensuring the time it takes for the transaction to be confirmed on-chain is crucial for the smooth operation of the financial system.

If transactions face unpredictable delays (whether due to malicious interference or chance), many applications will become unusable. For on-chain financial applications to be competitive, the blockchain must provide short-term transaction on-chain guarantees: as soon as a valid transaction is submitted to the network, it must be packaged and added to the chain as quickly as possible. Take on-chain order books as an example. An efficient order book requires market makers to provide liquidity by continuously placing buy and sell orders. The core challenge for market makers is to minimize the bid-ask spread (the difference between the buy and sell prices) while avoiding adverse selection risk due to price discrepancies with the market. To this end, market makers must constantly update their orders to reflect the latest market conditions. For example, when a Federal Reserve announcement causes significant fluctuations in asset prices, market makers need to immediately update their orders to the new prices. In this situation, if the transactions used by market makers to update their orders cannot be added to the chain in time, arbitrageurs will trade at outdated prices, causing them losses. Market makers are forced to widen spreads to mitigate this risk, leading to a decline in the competitiveness of on-chain trading platforms. A predictable on-chain transaction mechanism can provide reliable guarantees for market makers, enabling them to respond quickly to off-chain events and maintain the efficient operation of the on-chain market. The Gap Between Current Status and Needs: Currently, existing public blockchains can only provide relatively reliable final on-chain guarantees on a timescale of a few seconds. This level of guarantee is sufficient for payment applications, but it is far from adequate for a large number of financial applications that require real-time responses from market participants. Taking the order book scenario mentioned above as an example: for market makers, if arbitrageurs' transactions can be prioritized and included in earlier blocks, then the guarantee of "on-chain within seconds" is meaningless. Lacking strong on-chain guarantees, market makers can only hedge against higher adverse selection risk by widening price spreads and providing users with worse quotes. This makes on-chain trading less attractive compared to other trading venues with stronger guarantees. For blockchain to truly realize its vision of becoming a modern infrastructure for capital markets, developers must solve these problems to enable high-value applications such as order books to flourish on-chain. Where exactly does the difficulty lie in achieving predictability? Strengthening on-chain transaction guarantees on existing public chains to support such financial scenarios is extremely difficult. Currently, some protocols rely on a single node (i.e., the "block-producing node") that decides which transactions can be packaged within a specified time. While this design reduces the engineering difficulty of building high-performance public chains, it also creates a potential economic monopoly—block-producing nodes can extract value. Typically, during the window period when a node is selected as a block-producing node, it has absolute control over which transactions are included in the block. For public chains that support various financial activities, block-producing nodes are in a privileged position. If a node refuses to package a transaction, the user's only recourse is to wait for the next block-producing node willing to package that transaction. In permissionless networks, block-producing nodes naturally have an incentive to extract value, commonly known as Miner Extractable Value (MEV). MEV is far more complex than simply performing sandwich attacks on AMM transactions. Even a block-producing node delaying transaction packaging by only tens of milliseconds can generate huge profits and significantly reduce the efficiency of the underlying application. If the order book prioritizes only a subset of traders' transactions, it's unfair to all other participants. In extreme cases, malicious behavior by block-producing nodes can even cause traders to abandon the platform altogether. For example: When the Federal Reserve announces an interest rate hike, the price of ETH instantly drops by 5%. All market makers on the order book hastily cancel their existing orders and reorder at the new price. Simultaneously, all arbitrageurs submit trades, attempting to sell ETH according to outdated orders. If this order book operates on a protocol with a single block-producing node design, that node will wield immense power. It can directly select to review all market maker order cancellations, allowing arbitrageurs to reap huge profits; or it can not fully review cancellations, but delay processing them until the arbitrageurs' transactions are on the blockchain before allowing them to proceed; block-producing nodes can even directly insert their own arbitrage transactions, profiting entirely from price discrepancies. The two core demands are: censorship resistance and transaction anonymity. Faced with this advantageous position, active participation by market makers becomes unprofitable—they can be exploited whenever prices fluctuate. The problem essentially stems from the excessive privileges possessed by block-producing nodes in two aspects: block-producing nodes can review the transactions of any trader; block-producing nodes can view others' transactions and submit their own response transactions accordingly. Either of these problems can have disastrous consequences. An example can illustrate the problem. Suppose an auction has two bidders: Alice and Bob, and Bob happens to be the block producer of the block where the auction takes place. (Only two bidders are used for illustrative purposes; this logic holds true for any number of bidders.) The auction accepts bids during block generation, with the time interval set to t=0 to t=1. Alice submits a bid bA at time tA, and Bob submits a bid bB at time tB (tB > tA). Since Bob is the block producer, he always ensures that he bids last. Both Alice and Bob can read the continuously updated real price of the asset (e.g., the median price of a centralized exchange). Let the price at time t be pt. We assume that at any time t, both parties expect the asset price at the end of the auction (t=1) to be equal to the current pt. The auction rules are simple: the higher bidder wins, and the transaction is completed according to their bid. The Necessity of Censorship Resistance Now let's consider what would happen if Bob used his block-producing node advantage. If Bob could censor Alice's bid, the auction mechanism would fail entirely. He could simply bid an extremely low price and win, as all other bids would be blocked. This would result in a near-zero final auction profit. The Necessity of Transaction Anonymity A more complex scenario is that Bob cannot directly censor Alice's bid, but he can see it before bidding. In this case, Bob would employ a simple strategy: when bidding, he would check if the current price ptB is higher than bA. If so, he would bid slightly higher than bA; otherwise, he would abandon the bid. Through this strategy, Bob would subject Alice to continuous adverse selection. Alice could only win if, after the price update, her bid was higher than the asset's expected value. Every time she won an auction, she anticipated a loss, eventually leading her to simply stop participating altogether. Once all competitors were out of the game, Bob could then bid an extremely low price to win the auction, reducing his winnings to zero. The core conclusion of this case is: **auction duration is irrelevant.** As long as Bob could review Alice's bids, or see Alice's bids before he bids, the auction was destined to fail. This logic also applies to all scenarios of high-frequency asset trading, including spot trading, perpetual contracts, and derivatives exchanges: if block-producing nodes possessed the same power as Bob in this case, the entire market mechanism would collapse. For on-chain products serving these scenarios to be viable, block-producing nodes must never be granted such privileges. How do these problems arise in reality today? The above description paints a bleak picture for any on-chain transaction using a permissionless single block-producing node protocol. However, many such protocols still result in strong trading volumes on decentralized exchanges (DEXs). Why is this? Two forces actually offset the aforementioned problems: Block-producing nodes do not fully abuse their economic privileges because they typically have a significant vested interest in the success of the underlying public chain; and various applications have developed workarounds to reduce their vulnerability to these issues. While these two factors have allowed decentralized finance (DeFi) to function to date, they are not sufficient in the long run for on-chain markets to truly compete with traditional off-chain markets. On a public blockchain with substantial economic activity, obtaining block-producing qualification requires staking a large amount of tokens. Therefore, block-producing nodes either hold a significant amount of collateral themselves or possess a reputation sufficient to persuade other token holders to delegate their staking rights to them. In either case, large node operators are typically public entities, and their reputations are at risk. Beyond reputation, their collateral holdings also imply an economic incentive to drive the public blockchain forward. This is why we haven't currently seen block-producing nodes completely abusing market power as described above—but this doesn't mean these problems don't exist. On the one hand, relying on social pressure and long-term interests to constrain the goodwill of node operators is not a reliable foundation for the future financial system. As the scale of on-chain financial activity expands, the potential profit margin for block-producing nodes will also increase. The greater this potential, the more difficult it will be to constrain block-producing nodes through social means and make them act against short-term interests. On the other hand, the extent to which block-producing nodes abuse market power is a continuous range, from mild behavior to complete market destruction. Node operators can unilaterally and gradually test the waters, using their power to obtain higher returns. When some operators continuously exceed the acceptable boundaries of behavior, other nodes will quickly follow suit. The behavior of a single node may seem to have limited impact, but when everyone starts to imitate, the shock will be very significant. Perhaps the most illustrative example of this phenomenon is the timing game: block-producing nodes will delay the publication of blocks as much as possible while ensuring that the blocks remain valid in the protocol, thereby earning higher rewards. This leads to longer block times, and if nodes are too aggressive, blocks may even be skipped. Although the profitability of such strategies is well known, block-producing nodes initially chose not to participate in such games mainly to maintain the image of good governance of the public chain. However, this social balance is very fragile. Once a node operator begins to use such strategies to obtain higher returns without being punished, other nodes will quickly follow suit. Time-based game theory is just one example of how block-producing nodes can increase their rewards without completely abusing their power. They can employ many other methods to boost their own returns at the expense of the application. Individually, these methods might be within the application's tolerance, but eventually they will develop to the point where on-chain operating costs exceed the returns. Another factor maintaining the normal operation of DeFi is that applications move their core logic off-chain, only uploading the results. For example, all protocols requiring fast auction execution choose to complete them off-chain. These applications typically run the necessary mechanisms on a set of permissioned nodes to circumvent problems caused by malicious block-producing nodes. For instance, UniswapX executes its Dutch auctions for matching transactions off-chain on the Ethereum mainnet, and Cowswap likewise runs its bulk auctions off-chain. While this approach ensures application operation, it puts the underlying public chain and its core value proposition on-chain in a precarious position. If the application's execution logic is all off-chain, the underlying public blockchain will only become a simple settlement layer. One of the core advantages of DeFi is composability, but in a world where all execution occurs off-chain, these applications are naturally in a fragmented environment. Relying on off-chain execution also adds a new prerequisite to the trust model of these applications: the application not only needs the underlying public blockchain to function properly, but also depends on the availability of off-chain infrastructure to work correctly. How to achieve predictability? To solve these problems, the protocol needs to meet two major characteristics: stable transaction on-chain and sorting rules, and privacy protection before transaction confirmation. Core requirement one: Censorship resistance. We summarize the first characteristic as short-term censorship resistance. If a protocol has short-term censorship resistance, then any transaction that reaches an honest node can be guaranteed to be included in the next available block. Short-term censorship resistance: Any valid transaction delivered to any honest node on time will definitely be included in the next block. More precisely, let's assume the protocol operates based on a fixed clock, with blocks generated at fixed intervals, such as every 100 milliseconds. Then we need the following guarantee: if a transaction is delivered to an honest node at 250 milliseconds, it will definitely be included in the block generated at 300 milliseconds. Attackers should not have the power to arbitrarily filter transactions, selectively package or remove certain transactions. The core idea of ​​this definition is that users and applications should have highly reliable ways to ensure transactions are successfully uploaded to the blockchain at any time. A transaction should not fail to be uploaded to the blockchain due to packet loss from a single node—whether malicious or due to operational failure. While this definition requires providing on-chain guarantees for transactions delivered to any honest node, in practice, the overhead of achieving this may be too high. The core significance lies in the fact that the protocol should possess sufficient stability, ensuring highly predictable entry points for transaction on-chain processing and possessing simple and easy-to-understand logic. Permissionless single-block-producing node protocols clearly do not meet this requirement, because if the sole block-producing node acts maliciously at any given moment, the transaction has no other way to be on-chain. However, even if only one group of four nodes can guarantee that transactions are packaged within each time period, it will significantly increase the choices for users and applications to have transactions on-chain. Sacrificing some performance for reliability is worthwhile for the stable and prosperous development of applications. Finding a suitable balance between robustness and performance still requires further research, but the guarantees provided by existing protocols are far from sufficient. Once the protocol can guarantee transaction on-chain processing, the sorting problem will be readily solved. The protocol can adopt any deterministic sorting rule to ensure consistent sorting results. The simplest solution is to sort by priority fee, or to allow applications to flexibly sort transactions that interact with their state. The optimal sorting method for transactions remains an active research direction, but in any case, the sorting rule is only meaningful when transactions can be successfully on-chain. Core Requirement Two: Information Confidentiality After achieving short-term censorship resistance, another key characteristic that the protocol needs to satisfy is a privacy protection capability we call confidentiality. Confidentiality: Before a transaction is finally confirmed and uploaded to the blockchain by the protocol, no other participant besides the node receiving the transaction can obtain any information about the transaction. A protocol that satisfies confidentiality can allow receiving nodes to view all transactions submitted to them in plaintext, but requires the rest of the protocol to be completely unaware of the transaction content until consensus is reached and the order of transactions in the final log is determined. For example, the protocol can use time-lock encryption to make the entire content of a block invisible before a certain deadline; or it can use threshold encryption, decrypting the block only after the committee confirms that the block is irreversible. This means that a node might misuse the transaction information submitted to it, but other nodes in the network will not be able to see the content to be confirmed until consensus is reached. When the transaction information is made public to the rest of the network, the transaction has already been sorted and confirmed, so other participants cannot preemptively trade. For this definition to be practical, it requires that multiple nodes can complete the on-chain transaction at any given time. We did not adopt a stronger definition of privacy—that is, only the user knows the transaction content before confirmation (such as the encrypted transaction pool scheme)—because the protocol needs some step as a spam filter. If the transaction content is completely hidden from the entire network, the network cannot distinguish between spam and valid transactions. The only solution is to retain some non-hidden metadata in the transaction, such as the payment address, which deducts the fee regardless of whether the transaction is valid. However, this type of metadata may still leak enough information to give attackers an opportunity. Therefore, we prefer to design it so that a single node can see the complete transaction, but other nodes in the network cannot. However, this also means that for this feature to be effective, users must have at least one honest node as the entry point for transactions to be recorded on the blockchain during each time period.

Conclusion

Protocols that simultaneously possess short-term censorship resistance and information anonymity are ideal underlying foundations for building financial applications. Returning to our example of running an auction on the blockchain, these two features directly solve the problem that Bob might cause market collapse. Bob cannot censor Alice's bids, nor can he use Alice's bids to guide his own bidding, precisely solving problems 1) and 2) in the previous example.

With the guarantee of short-term censorship resistance, any user submitting a transaction—whether it's a trade or an auction bid—can ensure that the transaction is instantly packaged and recorded on the blockchain. Market makers can update orders, bidders can bid quickly, and clearing operations can be executed efficiently. Users can be confident that any action they initiate will be executed immediately. This will enable a new generation of low-latency real-world financial applications to be built entirely on-chain. For blockchain to truly compete with, or even surpass, existing financial infrastructure in performance, we need to address far more than just throughput issues.

data.title: US Congressional hearing pushes forward with security tokenization; Iran sets five conditions for ceasefire.
data.description:
Headline

▌US House Hearings Advance Securities Tokenization, SEC Proposes Innovation Exemption Program

On March 26, the US House Financial Services Committee held a hearing on securities tokenization. Republican Representative Andy Barr stated that securities tokenization is "undoubtedly imminent" and that regulatory modernization is imperative. The SEC has previously approved the DTCC to tokenize highly liquid assets on designated blockchains and recently approved Nasdaq to support tokenized stock trading. SEC Chairman Paul Atkins stated that he will solicit comments on an innovation exemption program to establish a regulatory sandbox for on-chain assets. Some Democratic lawmakers expressed concerns about the exemption program, believing it could create a two-tier market.

▌Iran sets five conditions for ceasefire, saying "accept conditions first, then negotiate"

According to Press TV, a news television station controlled by the Iranian government, Iranian government officials issued a statement saying that Iran has responded negatively to the US proposal and insists that the war will only end on Iran's own terms and timetable. This official, familiar with the details of the Iranian government, stated that Iran will not allow Trump to decide when the war ends. He also emphasized Tehran's determination to continue its defense and inflict "heavy blows" on the enemy until its demands are met. The official outlined five specific conditions for Iran to agree to end the war, including: ① A complete cessation of the enemy's "aggression and assassination" activities. ② The establishment of a concrete mechanism to ensure that war will not be imposed on Iran again. ③ Guarantee and clearly define the payment of compensation and repair costs for war losses. ④ An end to the war on all fronts and within the scope of all participating resistance organizations throughout the region. ⑤ International recognition and guarantee of Iran's sovereign right to exercise jurisdiction over the Strait of Hormuz.

▌Bloomberg ETF Analyst: Morgan Stanley Bitcoin ETF MSBT Receives Official Listing Announcement from NYSE

On March 26, Eric Balchunas, Senior ETF Analyst at Bloomberg, published an article on the X platform stating that the Morgan Stanley Bitcoin ETF MSBT has received an official listing announcement from the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE), which usually means an imminent listing.

▌Federal Reserve Governor Milan: Very Optimistic About Stablecoins

On March 26, Federal Reserve Governor Milan stated that he is very optimistic about stablecoins and will invest more funds into the dollar ecosystem.

**HyperEVM Ecosystem Stablecoin Market Cap Surpasses $1 Billion** March 25th news: "The total market capitalization of stablecoins on the HyperEVM network has officially surpassed the $1 billion mark, with Circle's USDC dominating and becoming the core driving force behind this growth." **Bitwise: Latest Draft of the CLARITY Act Causes Circle to Plunge 20%; Future Market Cap Review** Bitwise Chief Investment Officer Matt Hougan wrote that, based on conservative assumptions and even considering the recent concerns raised by the latest draft of the CLARITY Act, he predicts Circle's value will reach $75 billion by 2030. To date, interest income has not been the primary driver of stablecoin growth. Currently, the vast majority of stablecoins do not generate interest when held. Stablecoins have gained rapid popularity because they enable efficient and reliable global transfer of funds—for trade settlements, as loan collateral, as an alternative to unstable fiat currencies, and many other applications. Convenience is a core advantage of currencies, and this is precisely what stablecoins excel at. Currently, the average annualized interest rate for a typical US savings account is around 0.60%, and for a demand deposit account, it's only 0.07%. People don't deposit money in these accounts for interest. If the global financial system is increasingly migrating to the blockchain, I expect stablecoins to play an increasingly important role in this transformation, regardless of whether they offer interest. Based on my simple estimate—a market size of $1.9 trillion, a 25% market share, and a 0.8% profit margin—Circle's revenue will reach $3.8 billion by 2030, excluding distribution costs and other expenses. Currently, the company's actual operating expenses are relatively low ($144 million in 2025), meaning that even if these expenses double or triple by 2030, approximately $2.7 billion of revenue would still be net profit after taxes. Based on the current average price-to-earnings ratio (28) of the S&P 500, Circle's market capitalization would reach $75 billion. According to Arkham data, BlackRock transferred a total of 11,780 ETH, worth approximately $25.75 million, to the Coinbase Prime address through its Ethereum exchange-traded fund ETHA; and approximately 634 BTC, worth approximately $45.35 million, to the Coinbase Prime address through its Bitcoin exchange-traded fund IBIT. **14.16 Billion USD in Bitcoin Options Expire This Friday, Maximum Threat Price Locked in at $75,000** March 25th news: "This Friday at 4 PM, 199,000 Bitcoin options will expire on the Deribit exchange, with a notional total value of $14.16 billion. Current data shows the maximum pain price is $75,000, and the put/call ratio is 0.63." **Strategy to Launch Dedicated Bitcoin Security Program** March 25th news: Strategy will launch a dedicated Bitcoin security program, aiming to collaborate with global cybersecurity, cryptocurrency, and Bitcoin experts to strengthen the security, resilience, and credibility of the Bitcoin network. The company is seeking a Director of Bitcoin Security to lead this initiative. This role will involve serving as an in-house expert on protocols, cryptography, and asset custody, as well as a key external liaison for the industry. The position will comprehensively build Strategy's Bitcoin security framework, covering threat modeling, key management, multi-signature custody solution design, and wallet architecture, with a focus on institutional-grade resilience and asset recoverability. Strategy states that the initiative will bring together exchanges, custodians, asset managers, service providers, and core developers to address emerging threats, security vulnerabilities, and establish industry best practices. The initiative will also include internal training, executive consulting, and ongoing industry collaboration, driving large-scale improvements in Bitcoin security through regular meetings and public progress updates.

▌Bitmine Officially Launches Institutional-Grade Ethereum Staking Platform MAVAN

Nasdaq-listed Ethereum treasury company Bitmine announced the official launch of its institutional-grade Ethereum staking platform, MAVAN (Made in America Validator Network), designed to provide institutions with a highly secure, high-performance, and resilient staking infrastructure. MAVAN combines US-based validator nodes with a globally distributed architecture, meeting the needs of institutions requiring compliant local infrastructure while also supporting global client access. Initially designed to support Bitmine's own Ethereum asset allocation, the platform will be opened to institutional investors, custodians, and ecosystem partners in the future. As of March 24, 2026, Bitmine has staked approximately 3.1426 million ETH (approximately $6.8 billion).

Japanese and Australian stock index futures rose, while Hong Kong stock index futures fell slightly. US stock index futures were largely unchanged after the S&P 500 rose for the second time this week, gaining 0.5% on Wednesday; the technology-heavy Nasdaq 100 rose 0.7%.** The index of Chinese companies listed in the US rose 1.9%.

▌The probability of the Fed keeping interest rates unchanged in April is 94.8%

On March 26, according to CME's "FedWatch": the probability of the Fed raising interest rates by 25 basis points in April is 5.2%, and the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 94.8%. The probability of the Fed raising interest rates by a cumulative 25 basis points by June is 11.7%, the probability of raising interest rates by a cumulative 50 basis points is 0.4%, and the probability of keeping interest rates unchanged is 87.9%.

Golden Encyclopedia

▌Can Bitcoin still be called "digital gold" under geopolitical crises?

Every time there is a geopolitical crisis, the price of gold rises, and the price of Bitcoin plummets. After six tests, the claim of "digital gold" has never been confirmed by data.

Countries hoard gold but exclude Bitcoin from their reserves. For investors, Bitcoin exhibits an asymmetry: it falls with stocks but doesn't rise with them. Three structural asymmetries prevent Bitcoin from achieving safe-haven status: an overabundance of derivatives (market structure), the dominance of leveraged traders (participant composition), and a lack of repeatable behavioral records (behavioral accumulation). Bitcoin is not a safe-haven asset, but it is a "crisis-useful asset," capable of functioning when borders are closed and banks fail. If these three asymmetries narrow, Bitcoin may no longer be a replica of gold but a completely new "next-generation gold." Generational shifts and the widespread adoption of algorithms are key factors that could accelerate this process.

data.title: Behind the explosion of intelligent agents
data.description:
Author: Jordi Visser

Over the past three years, since the release of ChatGPT, my life has undergone a complete transformation, far exceeding any of my original expectations for the future. I still remember the day someone suggested I take a Python course to better master ChatGPT; and how that short three-hour YouTube tutorial utterly shattered my self-doubt—I had once lacked confidence and was unsure if I could truly do anything meaningful with computers. But despite all that progress, nothing compares to the sheer magnitude of change in my daily life since configuring my first OpenClaw. The thought of simply sending a message to an assistant on my phone to build this idea, reviewing it when I got home after a long day, or running an all-nighter and evaluating the results the next morning—it's been a game-changing experience.

This shift could create one of the most significant changes in the economic demand architecture in modern history: a leap from billions of human consumers to trillions of intelligent agent consumers. For centuries, technology has transformed production, labor, and distribution, but the ultimate buyers have always been humans. Industrialization replaced workers, but humans still bought goods. The internet eliminated physical stores, but humans still clicked to buy. In the next phase, this assumption will be broken. Increasingly, the direct buyers, coordinators, negotiators, and executors will no longer be humans, but intelligent agents.

Human consumers are limited by physiological functions, attention span, time, biases, emotions, and transaction friction. They need sleep. They hesitate.

They can only compare a few options and make imperfect decisions. Intelligent agents, on the other hand, can instantly compare thousands of variables, dynamically adjust, and continuously optimize until the transaction is complete. The vision of trillions of intelligent agent consumers is not an exaggeration of the future. It is the inevitable result of embedding intelligence into software, devices, platforms, vehicles, robots, and ultimately, humanoid robots. One person might control dozens of intelligent agents, while a company might deploy millions. A smart factory will operate as a dense network of intelligent agents, responsible for ordering parts, procuring electricity, allocating computing power, managing robotic workflows, and settling transactions between suppliers and logistics networks. Even if the human population remains constant, the number of economic participants will expand exponentially. This has dramatically impacted the labor market. Looking back at history… Technological disruption typically only replaces a portion of jobs on the supply side, while humans remain central to the demand side. However, the agent economy is entirely different because this disruptive wave sweeps across both supply and demand. Humans face pressure not only as workers but also as increasingly marginalized participants in transactions. A growing proportion of the economy is comprised of agent-to-agent transactions that require no human intervention. This doesn't mean humans will disappear entirely. The labor market will shift towards oversight, orchestration, anomaly handling, trust mechanisms, and high-dimensional decision-making. However, the traditional assumption that "job creation feeds back into a human-centric demand system" is becoming increasingly unreliable. In this new cycle, a larger proportion of demand may come from non-human participants operating with machine logic rather than family psychology. Modern economic analysis is largely built around human timescales. Growth, productivity, and GDP are all measured through a framework shaped by working hours, pay cycles, settlement delays, and household consumption behavior. The agent economy compresses time. It dramatically increases the speed at which work is performed, decisions are made, and transactions are completed. Time has always been one of the implicit constraints on economic growth, and agents break this constraint. This is where the velocity of money comes into play. By automating the negotiation and settlement of trillions of microtransactions, agents greatly increase the speed at which money flows through the system. The surge in nominal economic activity is not only due to more things happening, but also to things happening faster. Time compression begins to manifest macroscopically as accelerated growth. The Inevitability of Fiat Currency Friction and Embracing Programmable Money However, this acceleration has encountered a stumbling block. A layer of intelligent agents operating at near-infinite speed cannot cleanly and efficiently connect to the traditional financial infrastructure built around ACH, SWIFT, office hours, reconciliation delays, and human oversight. The faster the intelligent agent economy grows, the more glaring this friction becomes. Traditional financial tracks are laid out for a world where humans are the primary participants. They simply cannot support trillions of autonomous systems continuously settling value across borders and platforms. A world with trillions of intelligent agent consumers cannot operate on a trust system designed for slow human oversight. Without programmable safeguards, the risks become incalculable: runaway spending, Recursive feedback loops, automated fraud, and massive flash crashes that traditional financial institutions are simply powerless to handle. The future calls for a monetary and asset system native to a world of autonomous transactions by non-human participants. This is precisely the opportunity for cryptocurrencies to leap from a speculative sideline to a strategic infrastructure. Stablecoins enable real-time settlement. Smart contracts allow conditional execution. Wallets become operational accounts for smart agents. On-chain systems allow ownership, permissions, and collateral to be clearly identified by software. Machine commerce requires more than just speed. It requires programmable constraints: embedding rules directly into the transaction layer itself. In a machine economy, compliance, authorization, risk limits, and settlement logic cannot exist as slow, artificial add-ons outside the system. They must be part of the track itself. Tokenization and the Expanding Digital Economy In this future vision, Bitcoin's role differs from programmable money. It acts as a store of value. As I've pointed out before, it possesses an advantage that software investments in the fiat world lack: a moat as the preferred store of value in the digital economy. As the digital economy expands through trillions of transactions driven by intelligent agents, the digital asset ecosystem flourishes. Bitcoin benefits not because it processes machine-driven transactions, but because it anchors a value system in this increasingly digital world. The larger the digital economy, the more indispensable a scarce, rules-based, globally recognized digital reserve asset becomes. Bitcoin's value proposition grows stronger with the expansion of the economy it underpins. Tokenization further extends this logic. Today, vast pools of wealth exist in a relatively dormant state: real estate, Private equity, infrastructure, private lending. If trillions of intelligent agents need to conduct real-time transactions and continuously consume liquid collateral, then this capital can no longer remain static. Tokenization transforms these assets into extremely fine-grained digital units that can be identified, divided, pledged, and mobilized, thereby activating dormant, static wealth into active collateral that can flow freely within the financial architecture of the machine economy. The emergence of humanoid robots makes this vision even grander. Once intelligent agents possess physical forms, they will become direct participants in physical commerce: ordering parts, purchasing electricity, contracting logistics, and leasing warehouses. The machine economy will extend from cloud infrastructure to the physical world. The consumer at the forefront of this disruptive transformation will no longer be merely a human holding a mobile phone; it could be a machine holding a wallet. For investors, this is precisely where the real significance lies right now. The narrative of artificial intelligence is not just about smarter models or lower labor costs. It's about the birth of a completely new class of economic participants. OpenClaw is crucial because it heralds the arrival of the intelligent agent layer, not the distant future. Once this layer is fully deployed, the number of active economic participants will leap from billions to trillions. The gears of the economy will accelerate, the velocity of money will soar, the labor market will be forced to reshape, and traditional financial infrastructure will begin to crumble. This is why programmable money and digital assets are destined to reap the rewards; they are no longer products of speculative frenzy, but the underlying infrastructure of machine-native commerce. The next great economic transformation may not be defined solely by smarter software. Its watershed moment may well be the historic instant when consumers themselves cease to be human.
