data.title: Bitwise: The latest draft of the Clarity Act caused Circle's stock to plummet by 20%.
data.description:
Author:Matt Hougan, Chief Investment Officer, BitwiseTranslated by: Jinse Finance

Based on conservative assumptions, even considering the recent concerns raised by the Clarity Act, I project Circle's value to reach $75 billion by 2030.

One ​​of the most frequently asked questions I get is, "How should I invest in stablecoins?"

We typically advise people to consider crypto assets that support the stablecoin ecosystem, such as Ethereum, Solana, and Chainlink, and/or crypto companies that have established a presence in this space, such as Circle and Coinbase.

... Since it's difficult to predict who will benefit most from the rise of stablecoins, a reasonable strategy is to invest across the entire sector. However, among all options, one opportunity stands out: Circle—the issuer of USDC, the world's second-largest stablecoin. It's the only publicly listed, pure-play stablecoin company. To me, it's the most obvious choice. So, is it a good investment? Now is the opportune time to answer this question, as Circle's stock price has plummeted (Circle plunged 20% on March 24th): the latest draft of the Clarity Act restricts platforms' ability to pay interest to stablecoin users. But I believe this reaction is somewhat overreacting. To explain why, we need to examine Circle's future from a macro perspective.

Three Core Issues Determining Circle's Future

1. How Big Will the Stablecoin Market Be?

The first question is how big the stablecoin market will grow. There are various predictions on this, but the most widely cited comes from Citigroup. Their "baseline scenario" predicts that stablecoin assets under management (AUM) will reach $1.9 trillion by 2030; the "optimistic scenario" predicts $4 trillion.

The news regarding the Clarity Act has not changed this baseline prediction. To date, interest income has not been the primary driver of stablecoin growth. Currently, the vast majority of stablecoins do not generate interest when held. Stablecoins have become rapidly popular because they allow people to efficiently and reliably transfer funds globally—for trade settlements, as loan collateral, as an alternative to unstable fiat currencies, and many other scenarios.

Convenience is a core advantage of currency, and this is precisely what stablecoins excel at. Currently, the average annualized interest rate for ordinary savings accounts in the United States is about 0.60%, and for demand deposit accounts it's only 0.07%. People deposit money into these accounts not for interest. If the global financial system is increasingly migrating to the blockchain track, then regardless of whether stablecoins offer interest, I expect them to play an increasingly important role in this transformation. My intuition tells me that Citigroup's baseline scenario is actually quite conservative. However, to maintain the rigor of our analysis, we still use their $1.9 trillion forecast as the basis for our estimation. 2. What market capitalization share will Circle's USDC hold? Currently, Circle's USDC holds a 25% share of the stablecoin market, second only to Tether's USDT. (Why not invest in Tether? You can't. Because it's a private company.) Stablecoin Market Cap Distribution [Image of stablecoin market cap distribution] Source: Bitwise Asset Management, data from The Block. Time range: January 1, 2020 to March 23, 2026. Note: "Others" includes BUSD, CRVUSD, DAI, FDUSD, FEI, FRAX, GHO, GUSD, LUSD, MIM, PYUSD, TUSD, USDD, USDE, USDP, and USDS. A common view is that Circle's market share will gradually shrink as large companies like Bank of America, Stripe, and Wells Fargo enter the stablecoin space. I doubt this. Historically, innovators have often been good at maintaining early market leadership. For example: In 1976, a little-known company, Vanguard, launched the world's first index fund. Today, Vanguard manages more passive assets than any other institution in the world. In 1993, State Street launched the first U.S. exchange-traded fund (ETF)—SPY—at a time when it was not a giant in the asset management industry. Today, it remains the world's most actively traded ETF, managing over $650 billion in assets. In 1996, a little-known asset management firm, Barclays Global Investors (BGI), launched its first series of international ETFs. BGI was eventually acquired by BlackRock for $12 billion, and this small business eventually grew into iShares, now managing $5 trillion in assets. We've even seen initial signs that Circle can comfortably compete with established companies in the stablecoin space. In 2023, PayPal, one of the world's largest digital payments companies, launched its stablecoin PYUSD with great fanfare. However, the launch ultimately received a lukewarm reception, and PYUSD now holds just over 1% of the market share. Of course, in other sectors, there are also examples of giants crushing early entrants. For instance, in the money market fund sector, latecomers (such as Fidelity, Vanguard, and Federated Hermes) have taken a large share of the market from the original innovator (Reserve Fund Group). This is noteworthy, especially considering the similarities between money market funds and stablecoins: both absorb dollar funds and invest them in high-quality short-term securities like US Treasury bonds. Nevertheless, I still doubt that large banks will completely crush Circle. Circle's market share could very well expand. After all, while Circle holds "only" 25% of the overall stablecoin market, its share in the regulated stablecoin market is much larger. (Tether's USDT dominates the offshore market.) While exact figures for Circle's regulated market share are difficult to obtain, I estimate it to be over 80%. If you believe that most of the growth in stablecoin assets under management will come from these markets (as banks, fintech companies, and large enterprises opt for onshore, regulated stablecoins), then Circle's market share could well exceed its current 25%. However, for the purposes of this analysis, we'll again take a conservative approach. Let's balance these two forces and assume Circle will maintain a 25% market share in the future. 3. What will the profit margin be? The final question is undoubtedly the most challenging and crucial: How much return will Circle receive from deposits? Currently, Circle receives interest income from all the US Treasury bonds backing USDC. At current rates, this means a yield of approximately 4% on $80 billion in assets under management. However, this figure overstates Circle's actual revenue potential. You must consider the distribution fees Circle pays to attract assets under management. For example, USDC, co-developed with Coinbase, is the exchange's flagship stablecoin. As part of the partnership agreement, Circle pays all interest income generated by its USDC on the Coinbase platform to Coinbase, which then passes on a large portion to its users. Circle also has distribution agreements with other exchanges. Here, Circle is betting that by paying a portion of the distribution fees, it can launch a marketing flywheel to directly attract assets—which it can then generate more revenue from or monetize in other ways in the future. Overall, Circle currently pays about 60% of its revenue to its distribution partners. This means that at current interest rate levels, its effective "commission rate" is approximately 1.6%. Is this rate sustainable? There are two key factors to consider. The first is interest rates. Circle's interest income is directly linked to current interest rates. Fed rate hikes benefit Circle, while rate cuts harm it. The second is competition. Imagine a world with hundreds of stablecoins, where consumers frequently switch between products like USDC, WFUSD, BAUSD, and PYUSD. Circle's ability to retain interest income would be limited. Theoretically, competition compresses profit margins. This is a fundamental principle of Economics 101. However, I have my doubts. Markets that "should" be perfectly efficient are often not. Charles Schwab earns billions of dollars annually from the spread between the interest rates paid to depositors and the returns earned from using those funds—even though customers could easily switch to higher-yielding products with a click of the mouse. But customers don't always do this because its core value proposition isn't interest, but convenience, trust, and integration. In many ways, the same applies to USDC: people hold it because it's usable everywhere and trusted, not because of the interest rate. This stickiness won't disappear overnight. I would also add that the current draft of the Clarity Act could actually benefit Circle's profit margins by making it more difficult for stablecoin holders to earn interest income. Despite this, overall, I believe Circle's profit margins will face greater pressure in the future as competition intensifies. It may even have to change how it generates revenue from users, something the company is actively working towards. For this analysis, we assume the commission rate will be halved to 0.8%. Conclusion Answering these three questions doesn't cover every aspect of Circle's business. As I implied above, it has launched its own blockchain, continues to innovate in payment technology, and its non-interest income is growing rapidly. But I believe these three questions are the efficient 80/20 rule for analyzing this stock. Based on my simple estimate—a market size of $1.9 trillion, a 25% market share, and a 0.8% profit margin—Circle's revenue will reach $3.8 billion by 2030, excluding distribution costs and other expenses. Currently, the company's actual operating expenses are relatively low (US$144 million in 2025), meaning that even if these expenses double or triple by 2030, approximately US$2.7 billion of revenue would still be net profit after taxes. Based on the current average price-to-earnings ratio of the S&P 500 (28x), Circle's market capitalization would reach US$75 billion. This figure is interesting; it's roughly double its current market capitalization. That's quite good—but you might ask, given its volatility, is it worth it? I should point out that I chose conservative assumptions at every step of my analysis. If stablecoin growth reaches Citigroup's optimistic scenario, or Circle's market share expands further (as it has recently done), or the company maintains its current commission rate or finds new revenue streams, its value could be much higher. Ultimately, I can imagine Circle's value far exceeding my rough estimate for 2030, and I can also imagine it falling short. One thing I like about this analysis is that it shows the current valuation is reasonable. If stablecoins develop as expected, even with conservative assumptions, you'll still find Circle quite attractive.

data.title: Finance needs both speed and brakes.
data.description:
Small annoyances can sometimes save lives.

Think about the constant seatbelt reminder in your car. This incessant beeping is annoying, and many people have complained about it. But it's precisely this constant reminder that prompts countless people to buckle their seatbelts. And the result? According to the Insurance Institute for Highway Safety (IIHS), these constant reminders save approximately 1,500 lives each year in the United States alone. It truly is a lifesaver.

Small annoyances can sometimes save you a lot of money.

A frustrating phenomenon in modern banking is being suddenly interrupted when you think you've completed a wire transfer. You've entered the account number, routing number, and recipient's name. At this point, the bank doesn't immediately complete the transfer but pauses to confirm that the recipient's name matches the account information. This extra step disrupts the flow.

In the words of the product team, it's like friction. However, this pause has become one of the most effective ways to ensure payment security globally. The "Confirmation of Payee" service, provided by Pay.UK, enables individuals and institutions in the UK to make transfers and currently covers over 99% of transactions across various payment channels. The service's verification volume has grown from 14,000 transactions per month in June 2020 to over 70 million transactions per month in July 2025. It has reduced "account error" transactions by 59% and reduced financial losses for end users by 20% to 40%. This is crucial at a time when the financial industry has been striving for seamless transactions for over a decade. We've seen efforts like "one tap," "one swipe," and "click to transact," attempting to make funds flow silently in the background. The financial industry's instinct is often to view every pause as a flaw. As the financial industry has evolved, it has become increasingly obsessed with seamless integration. However, this evolution has repeatedly reminded us that certain so-called "friction" is actually a necessary brake to prevent systemic collapse. The Need for Brakes in Traditional Finance Today, the financial industry has embedded these restrictions into every new infrastructure it builds. In the United States, brokers with market access qualifications must implement risk control measures to limit their financial risk exposure and ensure regulatory compliance. When the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) passed Rule 15c3-5, it stated that the rule was designed to address the risks posed by automated, high-speed trading and to prevent unrestricted access to exchanges. The financial world repeatedly revisits this lesson for a simple reason: once the brakes fail, the damage often exceeds what institutions can withstand and recover from. In 1987, on Black Monday, the Dow Jones Industrial Average plummeted 22% in a single day. The Brady Committee recommended adding a pause button to the "circuit breaker" mechanism, stipulating that trading would be suspended for 15 minutes when the market fell by a certain percentage. Without these restrictions, Black Monday would have wiped out $1.7 trillion in global market capitalization in a single day. Adjusted for inflation, this loss is equivalent to more than $4.7 trillion today, exceeding the current GDP of Germany, the world's third-largest economy. These brakes taught the financial world that sometimes the only way to maintain speed is to briefly stop the machine. In other cases, a brief pause can solve the problem. In August 2012, Knight Capital Group suffered a software glitch, causing its computers to buy and sell millions of shares in just 45 minutes. This glitch caused a loss of $440 million in less than an hour, bringing the market maker to the brink of bankruptcy. Knight Capital Group optimized its system in pursuit of speed, which is crucial in market trading. However, an uncontrolled system without brakes, even the fastest one, will crumble instantly. The lesson is: the faster the system goes, the more important the braking mechanism becomes. The retail financial industry itself also faces numerous problems. For years, brokers have strived to make high-risk products easy to operate in order to drive retail user growth. They persisted, but ultimately lost trust. In its 2021 disciplinary action against Robinhood, FINRA noted that the company failed to conduct due diligence before approving clients for options trading and relied heavily on unregulated automated "approval bots." The investor protection nonprofit self-regulatory organization claimed that Robinhood's system approved clients based on inconsistent or illogical information. FINRA stated that the company's system allowed applicants with clearly questionable risk profiles to be approved. Robinhood's system is optimized to process applications quickly, avoiding waiting for potential customers. However, it lacks a meaningful pause between curiosity and security. It's fast, but without brakes. A Peculiar Case in Cryptocurrency The recent Aave-CoW incident in the cryptocurrency world has raised the need for braking mechanisms in the financial sector to a whole new level. On March 12, 2025, a user executed a $50 million exchange transaction through CoW Swap (a decentralized exchange (DEX) aggregator designed to protect users from bot front-running). The transaction was integrated into the front end of the DeFi protocol Aave. Due to insufficient liquidity, the user ultimately received only $36,930 worth of tokens, while paying out $50 million.

While Aave explained in its post-incident analysis that the user ignored explicit warnings about the impact of high prices, its founder and CEO, Stani Kulechov, posted on X that the Aave team "will look into how to improve these safeguards."

... Leaving aside the technicalities, it's clear that a fast interface allowed a disastrous trade to proceed too far before the system could react. While some might question the user's judgment and ignorance of warnings, this isolated incident, viewed as both convenient and counterproductive to the development of new financial infrastructure like blockchain, highlights the issue. If cryptocurrencies want to avoid repeating this mistake, the solution lies in building a smarter execution layer. Some decentralized finance (DeFi) trading protocols are already moving in this direction. For example, Definitive.Fi argues that large on-chain transactions shouldn't simply choose the technically feasible path for processing. They should be simulated before submission and tested against real-world market conditions, broken down into smaller portions if necessary, and routed through broader liquidity pools. Therefore, a good trading system should not only check its own ability to complete the trade but also the optimal path to fulfill the order. For any emerging infrastructure, trust and additional security are not optional features, especially in the financial sector. A product that makes transactions, lending, or fund transfers easy and convenient can certainly contribute to its rapid development, but a failure can have serious consequences. We have seen this pattern in all the traditional financial examples mentioned above. The system attempts to minimize visible friction points—even if these are necessary limitations—to mask its complexity and hopes that a smooth user experience will win over more consumers. However, confidence in the financial sector is rarely built this way. It often comes from financial institutions identifying critical moments requiring intervention and taking unpleasant but necessary steps to prevent such behavior. Pay.UK's payee verification mechanism is a case in point. While being repeatedly asked to confirm bank account names is certainly not a pleasant experience, it does prevent errors from causing potentially costly and irreparable losses. Stani of Aave understands this well. For this reason, he acknowledges that customers are not always clear about the order flow, who the payer is, or whether there are better transaction channels. This understanding is especially important in emerging industries like cryptocurrency and blockchain, where few users understand the technical processes of a transaction and the consequences of each click. In this context, acknowledging pain points and taking steps to address them is crucial for building consumer trust. The tricky part is that there's a fine line between a braking mechanism and random inconvenience and friction. A good braking mechanism doesn't completely slow things down, but rather applies slight resistance with precise timing. Taking the Aave-CoW case as an example, we can think of a good braking mechanism as an economic rationality check. It allows the system to scan more trading venues before routing, preventing order intent from falling into the hands of malicious actors, simulating outcomes before execution, and breaking down large transactions to avoid users being penalized for excessively large trades. These mechanisms are key to ensuring the trustworthiness of financial infrastructure. This distinction is important because there are still some pain points to be addressed in the financial sector. For example, cumbersome paperwork, inefficient compliance processes that slow down the entire process, hidden fees disguised as part of the process, and daunting registration processes all scare away new users. None of these should be justified. Setting up a "brake" isn't about justifying uglier products or pop-up ads; it's about creating a pause point when users are about to make irreversible decisions based on incomplete information. This is especially true when customers are handling large orders, selling high-risk products, exploring new payment methods, or making one-click operations (where risk is immediate and speed isn't the primary concern) during periods of low market demand. This also contains some business implications. The financial industry often talks about building safeguards only after a product and market fit. This is a misleading order. In finance, safeguards are an integral part of product and market fit. If implemented properly, safeguards won't even hinder the process. The Pay.UK case further illustrates that "recipient confirmation" is not a dispensable anti-fraud feature, but rather a "practical service that customers expect to see" when using the system for transactions. Emerging financial infrastructures, such as blockchain, aim to earn trust and withstand errors, scandals, and market pressures, just like traditional finance. But this is no easy task. It must proactively consider how to earn trust before earning users, because only by earning trust will users naturally follow. The reverse is not necessarily true. If blockchain can take strategic braking measures, its speed will surpass that of any other financial infrastructure.

data.title: From the brink of bankruptcy to a net worth of $4.2 billion, he defied the odds by borrowing money to buy cryptocurrency.
data.description:
A tech mogul who was once investigated by the SEC and lost $6 billion in wealth has now made a comeback on the rich list with a high-stakes gamble.

In August 2020, news shocked Wall Street and the crypto world: publicly traded company MicroStrategy announced it would use company funds to buy Bitcoin in large quantities.

At the time, the business intelligence software company's stock price had been sluggish for a long time, and the personal wealth of its founder and CEO, Michael Thaler, had already shrunk significantly from its peak during the dot-com bubble.

No one could have imagined that this decision would become the craziest and most successful reversal of his life.

The Fall of a Genius: From Billionaire to SEC Investigator Michael Thaler's story begins with a typical American Dream. Born in Nebraska in 1965, his father was an Air Force sergeant. With his exceptional intelligence, he received an Air Force scholarship to MIT, graduating with top honors and a double degree in Aeronautics, Astronautics, Science, Technology and Society. In 1990, he co-founded MicroStrategy with his MIT brother, Sanju Bansal. Riding the wave of the internet boom, the company rose rapidly. After its IPO in 1998, Thaler became a billionaire, with a net worth of $7 billion. However, fate dealt him a heavy blow in 2000. At the time of the dot-com bubble burst, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission launched an investigation into MicroStrategy's accounting practices. The investigation found that the company had prematurely recognized revenue, violating accounting standards. The company was forced to restate its financial statements for the previous two years, changing profits to losses. The stock price plummeted, and Thaler's personal wealth evaporated by approximately $6 billion overnight. He eventually settled with the SEC by paying $830,000 in personal refunds and a $350,000 fine. For years afterward, his wealth never returned to its peak, and MicroStrategy struggled to survive in the software industry. A desperate gamble: $5.4 billion in debt, all in Bitcoin. "After scientifically studying everything on Earth, I concluded that Bitcoin is the best inflation hedge." In 2020, Thaler made the life-changing decision. He began continuously investing MicroStrategy's corporate funds, even money borrowed through bond issuance, into Bitcoin. This was an extremely bold and controversial strategy. Mainstream media erupted in outrage, with criticism pouring in. But Thaler remained unmoved. He publicly predicted that Bitcoin's market capitalization would eventually surpass $100 trillion and stated that he would "buy Bitcoin whenever he had spare cash." From then on, MicroStrategy's stock price moved almost in tandem with Bitcoin's price, becoming a "proxy target" for traditional capital markets investing in Bitcoin. As of March 2026, the company, now renamed "Strategy," held a staggering 762,099 Bitcoins, worth over $54 billion, firmly holding the top spot among global listed companies in terms of Bitcoin reserves. The Ultimate Bet: Renaming, Resignation, All for Bitcoin Thaler's faith in Bitcoin completely reshaped him and his company. In 2022, due to a $917 million paper loss on the company's Bitcoin investments, he resigned as CEO and became Executive Chairman. However, he clearly stated that this would allow him to "focus more on the company's Bitcoin acquisition and holding strategy." In 2025, the company underwent a complete transformation, officially changing its name from "MicroStrategy" to "Strategy Inc." The new logo uses Bitcoin orange and a stylized "B," declaring itself the world's first "Bitcoin Treasury Company." Despite the name change, the company retained its Nasdaq ticker symbol, MSTR. This move is widely seen as a defining moment in Thaler's journey. A traditional software company has officially transformed into a hybrid entity connecting technology, capital markets, and Bitcoin accumulation. Controversy and Belief: Why a Favorite of Bitcoin Yet a Bearish View on Ethereum? Thaler is a well-known "perpetual bull" in the crypto world. However, he is highly critical of cryptocurrencies other than Bitcoin, especially Ethereum. At the 2022 Istanbul Blockchain Economy Conference, he publicly questioned the technical reliability and ethical soundness of Ethereum. He pointed out that Ethereum's three-to-four-year technical roadmap meant it "looks like it won't be completed or stable for the next 36 months." "Ethical soundness means I need to know that no one can change the protocol, including Vitalik. I need to know that no individual in the Ethereum Foundation can change the protocol, because if they could, it would become a security, and if it's a security, it can't become a global currency," Thaler added. This almost fundamentalist belief in Bitcoin, coupled with his rejection of other cryptocurrencies, constitutes his unique investment philosophy and makes him one of the most recognizable and controversial opinion leaders in the crypto space.

Wealth Rebirth

Behind the $4.2 Billion Net Worth

According to Forbes' Real-Time Billionaires Wealth Tracker, Michael Thaler's estimated net worth is $4.2 billion in 2026.

According to Forbes' Real-Time Billionaires Wealth Tracker, Michael Thaler's estimated net worth is $4.2 billion in 2026.

His wealth primarily comes from three parts: MicroStrategy/Strategy stock: As a co-founder and major shareholder, his wealth is deeply tied to the company's stock price. Bitcoin investment: He personally purchased 17,732 bitcoins in 2020 at an average price of $9,882 each. Executive compensation: As executive chairman, he still receives a generous salary and bonuses. It's worth noting that during the cryptocurrency market crash of 2023, he briefly fell off the Forbes billionaires list. However, he made a strong comeback as Bitcoin prices rebounded and the company's stock price rose. Now, Strategy has even set a more aggressive goal: to accumulate up to 1 million Bitcoins by the end of 2026. If achieved, this would further tighten the circulating supply of Bitcoin and potentially reshape how institutions allocate the asset. In early 2025, Michael Thaler graced the cover of Forbes magazine, dubbed the "Bitcoin Alchemist." The cover story highlighted his audacious financial strategy of issuing $7.3 billion in debt to accumulate 471,107 bitcoins. From a darling of the internet age to a figure embroiled in accounting scandals, and then to the builder of a Bitcoin empire, Thaler's life has been a rollercoaster ride. Now, he sits atop 762,099 bitcoins, his sights set on reaching 1 million. While everyone else seeks safe haven, true adventurers choose to forge their ships amidst the waves. This high-stakes gamble is far from over, and the world is watching.

data.title: Beware of being cheated by fake new coin escrow apps like Safew, which risk stealing your coins.
data.description:
Safew is a privacy-focused messaging app similar in function to Telegram. Based on Telegram's encryption technology (MTProto protocol), messages, voice, video, and files are encrypted throughout the transmission process; only the two parties in the chat can see the content, and the server cannot read it. Some companies, for privacy reasons, even further utilize private deployments to completely control data or evade compliance reviews.

Due to Telegram's increasingly frequent law enforcement collaborations and community bans, NewCoin Guarantee, currently Southeast Asia's largest illegal cryptocurrency transaction escrow platform, is attempting to migrate Telegram public group merchants to Safew. This has led to a proliferation of fake Safew applications, posing a threat to the encrypted fund security of black market operators, primarily those in public group chats.

This article aims to partially expose this situation of black market manipulation.

Timeline On May 13, 2025, Beijing time, two of Southeast Asia's largest illegal cryptocurrency trading platforms, Haowang Guarantee and Xinbi Guarantee, were simultaneously sanctioned by Telegram. Numerous official customer service accounts and business groups of both entities were directly banned, causing a short-term halt to operations and widespread panic within the black market. The two entities responded differently—On the morning of May 13, Haowang Guarantee announced the cessation of operations and the transfer of all its public group business to Tudou Guarantee, an affiliated entity in which Haowang Guarantee had previously invested 30%. By nominally going bankrupt, Haowang Guarantee successfully shed its shell, rebranding as Tudou Guarantee, and continued its illegal business. On May 14th, Xinbi Guarantee updated its official website xinbi[.]com, announcing the official launch of the Safew public group to circumvent Telegram's ban on its illegal business groups. Although the official website content is no longer valid, traces can still be found using web archive tools: https://archive.md/iDXW8. Subsequently, voices of condemnation began to emerge in the black market community regarding the launch of Safew, a new cryptocurrency guarantee platform, which was accused of aiming to steal users' crypto assets. These negative discussions reached their peak in early 2026 after Potato Guarantee completely collapsed and the new cryptocurrency guarantee platform accelerated its migration to public groups. Despite repeated emphasis from Jinse on the correct download address for Safew and claims that the software is available on the iOS App Store, numerous fake Safew groups continue to create counterfeit, unofficial download websites and pollute search engine keywords for promotion.

Taking the unofficial link safew-x[.]com as an example.When analyzing the sample (download link:https://www.safew-x[.]com/_dl.php?t=win) using the ANY.RUN online security sandbox detection tool, malicious behavior was detected.https://www.safew-x[.]com/_dl.php?t=win

Sample execution releases Gh0stRAT SweetSpecter variant (full-featured remote access Trojan), establishes command and control communication with the C2 server, and triggers the following Emerging Threats rules: ET MALWARE [ANY.RUN] Gh0stRAT.Gen Server Response (SweetSpecter) ET DROP Spamhaus DROP Listed Traffic Inbound group 2 This variant possesses capabilities such as remote desktop access, keylogging, and file theft. Once the target device is infected, attackers can achieve complete remote control of the infected host, including real-time remote desktop access, keylogging, camera/microphone monitoring, file theft and dissemination, arbitrary command execution, and further deployment of malicious tools. Once infected, the threat actor can remain hidden for an extended period and steal sensitive data. It has been identified as a high-risk Remote Access Trojan (RAT). For merchants and users in public groups who heavily utilize cryptocurrency wallets for shady and illegal activities, the target of this type of malware is clearly the private keys of the wallets stored on the devices. Bitrace has long monitored the financial activities of Xinbi Guarantee. An investigation into the Safew public group's staking addresses shows that although Xinbi Guarantee launched the Safew public group in May 2025, it only allocated a separate business address for this service in August of that year, and the business scale was low and decreased month by month.

Until the end of 2025 and the beginning of 2026, Huiwang Payment and Tudou Guarantee went bankrupt one after another. Xinbi Guarantee vigorously promoted its Safew public group business, and address activity increased. In January 2026, it briefly achieved a monthly capital inflow of more than 32 million USDT, which then decreased month by month. After analyzing all staking addresses for new coin escrow, it was found that the staking volume of the Safew channel in one month was only equivalent to that of the Telegram channel in one day, indicating that Telegram is still the preferred choice for merchants in black and gray market public groups for new coin escrow. In conclusion, double-crossing among those involved in the black market is extremely frequent. From fake wallets and fake Telegrams to offline wrench attacks and online social engineering, this group operating outside the bounds of the law is becoming a target. Following the collapse of Potato Guarantee, NewCoin Guarantee has become the largest illegal cryptocurrency trading escrow platform in Southeast Asia. This phishing campaign targeting merchants in the Safew public group is not the beginning, nor is it the end. Bitrace will continue to monitor the situation.

data.title: a16z Deep Dive into Revolut: A Growth Engine for Continuous Compound Interest
data.description:
Source: a16z; Compiled by: Jinse Finance

As growth investors, we often say that great companies begin with their financial data. Take Revolut as an example. As a British company, they are required to publish annual financial data—and their data, to put it mildly, is outstanding:

Revenue grew by 46% to £4.5 billion.

Pre-tax profit increased by 57% to £1.7 billion, with a profit margin of 38%.

Retail customer growth was 30%, adding 16 million new customers in 2025 alone.

Revolut has a high market penetration in Europe, with no country having a fee income ratio exceeding 25%.

Revolut has a high penetration rate in the European market, with no other country having a fee income ratio exceeding 25%.

Similarly, revenue is currently distributed across six business segments, with no single segment accounting for more than 22% of total revenue. Eleven different product lines generate over £100 million in revenue. Despite its high capital adequacy ratio, its return on equity (ROE) is a remarkable 35%, far exceeding that of its peers. Revolut continues its rapid and efficient growth – its 75% growth rate (defined as revenue growth + net profit margin) is among the highest in modern, established financial institutions. More importantly, we believe Revolut still has significant room for growth in its existing markets, both in terms of user growth and profitability. Not to mention the potential, readily available new markets – Revolut has just applied for a US license and has truly global ambitions. The key point is: this isn't the kind of new-age bank your grandmother's generation remembers. Revolut has the potential to become one of the world's largest banks. While there's still a lot of work to be done to achieve this, we believe the foundation is laid. Let's begin. Let's look at the revenue figures first. Revolut's revenue growth is phenomenal. Along with NU (Nubank), Revolut stands out in the consumer fintech industry (as shown in the chart below). Since surpassing $1 billion in revenue in 2022, Revolut has achieved an astonishing 76% CAGR (or 70% in pounds sterling) over the following four years, making it one of the fastest-growing companies after reaching $1 billion in revenue. This growth is particularly noteworthy considering Europe's highly developed consumer banking sector (unlike the less developed markets where NU operates).

Source: Revolut 2025 Annual Report, a16z Analysis. Revenue is converted to USD at year-end exchange rate. NU revenue has deducted interest and expected credit losses.

In other words, in 2022, Revolut's revenue was lower than (or equal to) any of Robinhood, Affirm, SoFi, Adyen, Wise, or Chime. Now, its revenue is 33% to nearly three times higher than these well-known consumer fintech companies.

II. AnalysisRevolutGrowth Algorithm: Six Winning Strategies (and More)

A key advantage of Revolut is that it no longer has a single source of revenue. It has multiple revenue drivers, and these factors are all working simultaneously.

Revolut was initially designed to address a pain point for Europeans: foreign exchange fees. With Revolut, Europeans traveling to and from the Eurozone or sending money abroad no longer need to face payment delays or the 5% fee charged by banks.

What was once a single-product, geographically focused, and optional service provider has evolved into a full-fledged commercial and personal bank. In Europe (Revolut's current main operating region), Revolut accounts for approximately one-third of new account openings: [Image of Revolut profile: https://img.jinse.com.cn/7442044_watermarknone.png](https://img.jinse.com.cn/7442044_watermarknone.png) [Image of Revolut profile: uuEsH29uNpGjbdmbiQH62u7moIlKHhTjZW2j0vzy.png) ... Respondents are required to specify the institution where their accounts are held and the opening date of each account. In Europe, one in five working-age adults now use Revolut. Revolut's popularity across Europe reflects the company's impressive product speed and execution. Revolut offers a complete suite of personal and corporate banking services, driving its growth in various European markets. More importantly, Revolut's product suite is increasingly appealing to Eurozone users who may not care about its initial forex trading value proposition. We would have liked to call Revolut's platform "feature-rich," but Revolut continues to introduce new features, so that assessment may not be sufficient. Revolut's strength lies not only in the number of new features and products it offers, but also in the quality of its execution. Customers rave about it. A report released by the company in 2024 showed that 65% of new customers came through organic growth or referrals from existing customers. Our research also shows that Revolut's Net Promoter Score (NPS) is more than double the industry average.¹ Taking all factors into account, the end result is that the number of users continues to grow at a compound annual growth rate of 30%, reaching 68 million by the end of 2025. Source: Revolut Annual Report. To better understand these 68 million users, we can refer to the case of JPMorgan Chase (the world's largest bank outside of China): JPMorgan Chase has approximately 85 million consumer customers (of which over 70 million are considered "digitally active users"). It's worth noting that JPMorgan Chase's total assets under management are orders of magnitude larger than Revolut's, but in terms of user reach, Revolut is no longer just a "challenger"; it has become a true industry giant. Revolut's user base exceeds the combined total of SoFi, Robinhood, Dave, and Chime. A complete product portfolio not only continues to attract more customers but also creates an increasingly diversified revenue structure: Source: Revolut Annual Report. The company's six publicly disclosed main revenue streams are: interest income; debit card payments; wealth management; FX; subscriptions; and other revenue. All six business segments achieved year-over-year growth, and no single business segment accounted for more than 22% of revenue. The company's business diversification extends far beyond the figures mentioned above, as each revenue stream may encompass multiple sub-products (for example, its wealth management business covers public equities and cryptocurrencies). By 2025, it will have 11 different product lines generating over £100 million in revenue. Notably, 76% of its revenue comes from fees, an increase of over 4 percentage points from 2024, while interest income accounts for less than 22%. This contrasts sharply with traditional banks where over 70% of revenue comes from interest, which is one reason why Revolut achieves a high return on equity (more on this below). Unsurprisingly, this diversified revenue base translates into diversified ARPU growth.

Source: Revolut Annual Report, a16z Analysis. ARPU is defined as revenue per product line / average revenue per user during the period.

Since 2022, all reported revenue sources have grown, with total ARPU increasing by approximately 65%, or a compound annual growth rate of 18%.

Diversification is crucial because it helps to sustain compound growth and enhance resilience. Any year can see explosive growth in a product line, or it can face adversity (such as last year's declining interest rates).

Overall, however, the addition of new products and the continued growth in market share of core products still drive strong growth in average revenue per user (ARPU). Revolut has demonstrated rapid user growth, extremely fast product iteration, and diversified revenue streams, but we are also committed to improving efficiency. In 2025, Revolut achieved 46% revenue growth and a 29% net profit margin, resulting in an X-rule (growth rate + profit margin) of 75%. Now the 40 rule no longer applies!

Data source: Publicly available financial data provided by CapIQ, a16z analysis.

For companies that have not yet released financial reports, the data is 2025 A data or current analyst forecasts. Bubble size represents total revenue in 2025. NU revenue has deducted interest and expected credit losses.

This company combines growth and efficiency, placing it among the top in the industry—only a very few companies have achieved the 75% rule with revenue exceeding $1 billion.

In fact, considering that Robinhood and Dave are projected to grow by less than 30% next year (according to consensus), Revolut may soon take the lead. Efficiency is in Revolut's DNA. The company prioritizes developing its own banking infrastructure, drives highly organic growth, and rigorously controls costs, resulting in a net profit margin of 29%. Coupled with a limited number of physical branches, Revolut currently has a significant cost advantage over traditional banks, an advantage that will continue to strengthen as the company grows. Finally, the company also demonstrated how artificial intelligence can further improve operational efficiency. For example, in customer service: In 2024, Revolut's chatbot assistant reduced problem-solving time by 80%. In 2025, this improvement continued, with retail customer issue resolution times reduced by over 40% and enterprise customer issue resolution times reduced by over 50%—while Net Promoter Score (NPS) increased by nearly 12 percentage points year-over-year. Revolut's assistants are now able to resolve over 75% of customer inquiries. All these efficiency gains have enabled Revolut to achieve the highest scalable ROE we've seen in the fintech space (and it continues to improve). We've previously discussed the importance of ROE for bank valuations, and Revolut is a prime example of scalable, efficient operations. Source: Public financial data provided by CapIQ, analyzed by a16z. Return on equity (ROE) is defined as net profit in 2025 divided by average equity during that period. Revolut's ROE is as high as 35%, far exceeding other leading consumer fintech companies and approximately 3-4 times that of mature banks. It should be noted that Revolut has a high capital adequacy ratio (i.e., its published equity is higher than the capital adequacy ratio requirements of banks), which means that its "true" ROE may be even higher. It's hard to imagine growth efficiency reaching this level. IV. Ample Room for Growth: ARPU x Number of Users While Revolut's performance in 2025 was impressive, we believe it still has significant room for growth. Looking back at the company's core revenue growth algorithm (number of users x ARPU), both of these growth drivers have considerable room for improvement. The company reports that it will reach 68 million users by the end of 2025. As mentioned above, this is substantial, but it represents less than 15% of the approximately 450-500 million adult population in Europe (excluding Russia). Furthermore, this does not include the growth potential in Australia and Singapore (existing markets), Mexico and Brazil (recent entrants), the United States (recently applied for a banking license), and many more regions in the future. The key is that Revolut still has many more potential users to attract. Furthermore, the current user demographics suggest that the future is unlikely to be the same. Unsurprisingly, Revolut's user base is young and digitally savvy—we believe these customers represent the ultimate needs of most people. Source: a16z European Banking Survey, February 2026 (N=4,200). The survey markets include the UK, Ireland, France, Spain, Italy, Germany, and Poland. As Revolut continues to win over a large number of first-time banking customers (and convinces its older user base that banking can indeed provide a pleasant user experience), its market share should continue to grow. It's worth noting that, according to our research, approximately 25% of Revolut users under the age of 35 use Revolut as their primary account. We will discuss this in more detail later, but regardless of other factors, the maturation of this group alone should have a profound impact on the future of market share in the European banking sector. ARPU also has significant growth potential. Another growth driver—ARPU (Average Revenue Per User)—has even greater room for growth. Changes in customer wallet share in the financial services sector typically take decades, not years. Revolut continues to earn customer trust, with its primary account users (according to company reports) growing by 45%, exceeding the overall user growth rate of 30%. Rapid growth in primary account users is crucial because, in terms of average revenue per user (ARPU), primary account users represent the greatest benefit: Our research shows that existing banking institutions (with established customer relationships) can increase their primary account share to over 60%. Similarly, Revolut primary account users report that they spend and save approximately twice as much on their primary accounts as on any other account they have—and spending tends to increase as customers age; In short, more (and more established) primary users translate to higher ARPU, and Revolut's growing primary share has a fairly high ceiling, comparable to existing banks. Another aspect of Revolut's growing "primary" relationship is its untapped loan revenue opportunities: As mentioned above, Revolut currently derives 76% of its revenue from fees, compared to approximately 30% for established banks; and as of the end of 2025, Revolut's loan-to-deposit ratio (LDR) was only around 6%, while traditional banks typically have LDRs of 70% to 90% or more (approximately 4% based on total customer balance). Revolut's loan balance is projected to roughly triple by 2025 and will continue to grow for many years to come. Admittedly, robust loan growth takes time. However, based on current ceilings, Revolut has ample opportunity to further increase its average revenue per user (ARPU) by leveraging its balance sheet and offering customers higher-quality loan products. In comparison, a simple estimate of Barclays' UK consumer and corporate banking business suggests that its ARPU is around £435, approximately six times that of Revolut's current ARPU. V. Conclusion: No Longer Just a Challenger

Revolut's 2025 data is important not only because it is impressive, but also because it paints a complete picture of a financial institution, not just a "challenger" bank.

Customer growth remains strong, the profit model continues to expand, master account adoption continues to rise, and profitability continues to improve, while the company continues to invest and expand rapidly. This combination is extremely rare in the financial services industry (or indeed in any industry).

There is still much work to be done—particularly in lending, regulation, and expanding into new markets—but after reading this annual report, we believe the question is less about “Can Revolut become a scalable banking platform” and more about “How large can this platform grow?” The company’s announced long-term goal is “100 million daily active users in 100 countries.” Currently, this goal is progressing steadily.
